---
title: "Sustainable AI for Primary Care"
subtitle: "Designing Systems That Learn Without Leaving Communities Behind"
author: "Sean Sylvia, Ph.D."
institute: "DHEPLab, UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health"
date: today
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: images/adri-title-bg.jpg
  data-background-size: cover
  data-background-opacity: "0.25"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, dheplab-theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    chalkboard:
      buttons: true
    preview-links: true
    logo: images/dhep-logo-2.png
    footer: "Asia Development Research Institute | Technology for Sustainable PHC"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
---

## The Promise: AI-Enabled Primary Healthcare

::: {.eyebrow}
SETTING THE STAGE
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### What AI Can Do

- Extend specialist expertise to frontline workers
- Standardize care quality across settings
- Enable continuous learning from practice
- Reduce diagnostic delays
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### The Sustainability Question

- Can AI systems improve **and** remain equitable over time?
- Will they work for **hard-to-reach** populations?
- How do we design for **long-term** rather than short-term gains?
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
[~1 min] Set up the tension: AI has transformative potential, but sustainability requires thinking beyond initial deployment.
:::

---

## The Problem: AI Systems Can Degrade Over Time

::: {.eyebrow}
CASE STUDY
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
### Epic Sepsis Model

- Deployed in **56% of US hospitals**
- Initial promise: Early detection, save lives
- Reality: **Missed 67% of sepsis cases**
- Alert fatigue became widespread

::: {.small}
*Wong et al., JAMA Internal Medicine 2021*
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-warning}
### Pattern

Technical validation ≠ Sustainable real-world success
:::
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
[~45 sec] Concrete example showing the gap between promise and reality. Sets up WHY we need a different approach.
:::

---

## Why? Feedback Loops Change Everything

::: {.eyebrow}
CORE INSIGHT
:::

![](images/crisp-dm-slide2.svg){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
### Key Insight

AI deployment changes the data-generating process it depends on
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
[~1.5 min] Core theoretical contribution. When you deploy an AI system, clinician behavior changes, patient selection changes, and the training data for the next iteration is different from what came before.
:::

---

## The Equity Trap: Who Gets Left Behind?

::: {.eyebrow}
EQUITY IMPLICATIONS
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### The Feedback Loop

1. AI recommends treatment
2. Clinicians follow selectively
3. Outcomes become censored
4. **Disparities amplify over time**
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-important}
### The Paradox

Populations with **less** AI access may experience **more stable** predictions

- Urban/high-adoption → severe model drift
- Rural/hard-to-reach → preserved accuracy
:::
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
[~1 min] Surprising finding: populations we're trying to help can experience worst feedback effects. Hard-to-reach may be protected by lower adoption.
:::

---

# Design Principles {.section background-color="#13294B"}

## Design Principles for Sustainable AI in PHC

::: {.eyebrow}
RECOMMENDATIONS
:::

::: {.incremental}
1. **Community Engagement as Methodology**
   - Communities detect when systems stop working for them

2. **Design for Behavioral Adaptation**
   - Clinicians *will* change behavior—plan for it

3. **Continuous Validation, Not One-Time Approval**
   - Build monitoring from day one

4. **Equity-First Design**
   - Test on hard-to-reach first—if it works for them, it works for all
:::

::: {.notes}
[~1 min] Actionable recommendations connecting to panelists: community engagement (Ye Zhang), PHC sustainability (Krishna Rao), hard-to-reach (Weiming Tang).
:::

---

# Applications {.section background-color="#13294B"}

## Example 1: Sentinel — Information Markets for Surveillance

::: {.eyebrow}
SUSTAINABLE SURVEILLANCE
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Challenge

Traditional surveillance is **slow**, **expensive**, and misses **hard-to-reach communities**

### Solution: Information Markets

- Community members report health signals
- **Peer prediction** validates without ground truth
- **Fair compensation** creates sustainable participation
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
::: {.card}
### Pilot: Kibera, Kenya (2026)

- Partnership with CFK Africa
- Community health workers as information providers
- **Community-owned data ecosystem**
:::
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
[~45 sec] Sustainable design: community members are partners, not data sources. Fair compensation creates sustainable participation.
:::

---

## Example 2: CareLoom — CHW-Delivered MCH

::: {.eyebrow}
MATERNAL & CHILD HEALTH
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Challenge

Pregnant mothers in LMICs lack quality guidance; CHWs lack consistent tools

### Solution: CareLoom Platform

- Mobile app for CHW home visits
- Evidence-based curriculum delivery
- **Built on existing CHW networks**—no new infrastructure
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
::: {.card .featured}
### Evidence

- **China RCT**: Complete, manuscript in progress
- **Kenya**: Feasibility assessment underway

**Sustainability:** Leverages existing health system infrastructure
:::
:::
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
[~45 sec] Sustainable scaling: leverage existing CHW networks rather than building parallel infrastructure.
:::

---

## A Framework for Action {.dark-slide background-color="#13294B"}

::: {.eyebrow style="color: #7BB8E0;"}
STAKEHOLDER ACTIONS
:::

| Stakeholder | Sustainable AI Action |
|-------------|----------------------|
| **Policymakers** | Require continuous monitoring, not just approval |
| **Implementers** | Budget for ongoing validation, not just deployment |
| **Researchers** | Develop methods for detecting feedback loops |
| **Communities** | Partner in design and ongoing evaluation |

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
### The Goal

AI systems that get *better* over time, for *everyone*
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
[~1 min] Clear action points for different stakeholders. This provides the "specific action points for sustainability" the chair is looking for.
:::

---

## Thank You

::: {.eyebrow}
KEY MESSAGE
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
### Core Takeaway

**Sustainable AI for primary healthcare requires designing for the socio-technical system, not just the algorithm.**

- Account for feedback loops
- Engage communities as partners
- Monitor equity continuously
- Plan for behavioral adaptation
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](images/dhep-logo-2.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

**Sean Sylvia, Ph.D.**

sean.sylvia@unc.edu

[dhep.org](https://dhep.org)
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
[~30 sec] Closing. Leave with the core message that connects to the panel theme.
:::
